item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry, GoI-0432

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So GoI-4321 is distributing SCP-610?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Cool skip! I really liked the wordbuilding in this one, quick question, are you actually russian? I liked the descriptions of the anomalous crime syndicate and how they perverted sarkicism to their greed and bloodlust - we all know sarkicism is a nasty religion, but I can't help but think that there is a grain of truth to what Five told our deepcover agent.

I would have really liked to know what happened between the last memo and the raid on Moscow - how did the Black Lodge exactly discover they were being infiltrated, why did they kill the Twelve mothers, and what happened to the agent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My first thought while reading this wasn't werewolves. I thought of Buddy Love from the Nutty Professor. This SCP evokes strong visuals and I have a good sense of how this GOI operates. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Amazing world building that makes the entire article so much fun to read. I just started reading about Sarkicism and this is my favorite piece so far. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Masterfully written! High marks all around,from adding lore to the Sarkics, a fanciful location to explore, and several distinct and interesting characters! +100

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was very good, and I feel sorry for "Five".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvoted for the rape, or more specifically the "justification" of sorts that the main character uses for it. Something about the desperate need to prove hes still sort of a good guy feels worse than having it in in the first place

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I recently read through your Sarkic SCPs and I think they may be the best body horror on the site - they have a great feel of ridiculous, over-the-top nastiness and villainy while still managing to remain chillingly grounded and believable. In lieu of posting on all of them I'm posting on this one as it's probably my favourite, also mostly due to the worldbuilding. There is an awful lot of background crammed into this, but it doesn't intrude on the story at all. Excellent stuff. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really cool. What I like so much about this is the worldbuilding - it's interesting to see how people have adapted around these anomalies and incorporated them into their otherwise non-anomalous business. My favourite part, though, was the undercover investigation and agent. I would seriously read an entire book just about that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Displays fine on my PC.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The image in the last collapsible is broken.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this quite a bit, but I must say: the payoff of the final collapsible is a bit weak. I get it's some big stuff in the greater context of the Sarkic mythos, and it doesn't really come out of the left field nor fail to add to the article itself, but it ultimately feels like not that much would be lost if it was cut from the article.

But that's a ultimately a tiny complaint when the rest of the article does what it does so damn well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a valid Russian name (generic, but generic names are popular), but there's no reason for his klikuha to be "зверь" if it could be "волк" and dmitri "volk" volkov will sound dumb.

Зятьков -> Зятёк, Ящерицын -> Ящер, etc.

Bratva does not like to pass up chances to make nicknames out of surnames if they sound good, especially not in favor of a nickname that means the same thing.

Here's a list of dudes on random thief-in-law's funeral (i honestly googled отари владислав вор в законе):

Ahmadeev, Vasin, Garipov, Gvadjaya, Goglichidze, Kalashov, Lunev, Moniava, Parzklahadze, Tvalchrelidze, Chkhishvili, Yakunin, Alshibaya. Half of these are Georgian. Caucasians in Russian mafia are a giant "check this shit out, I did my research!" sign.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
IMO Dmitri Volkov is a perfectly valid Russian name, and "Beast" seems a fitting enough nickname for a gang leader, so no qualms about that.
Although I agree with you that a Georgian name contributed more to the way the article feels.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dmitri "Zver" Volkov?

Dmitri is the most popular Russian name, okay, but now it's an evil guy named "Beast" Wolf. Does he unsheath katana and *teleport behind u*? I like what you did with him being Georgian, though it was grammatically off. Really added authenticity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! I'll fix that!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The research you did on the Russian terms, especially the criminal stuff, is surprisingly good. Props on choosing the Golyanovo district (quite unsafe, dirty and criminal). Still, there are some nitpicks I'd like to point out:

Ярость

If you're aiming for one of the 7 deadly sins, it's probably "Гнев".

Фонари крaсныеi.

First off, the letter "i" at the end is unnecessary (it doesn't exist in Russian alphabet at all), plural for "red" is "красные". Second, the words should be the other way around, "Красные фонари". The way you put it, it's like "The lanterns red", sounds a bit lofty and poetic, which is probably not the way to go with adult club names.

Otari "Zver'" Vladislav

Otari is a Georgian first name. Vladislav is a Slavic first name. If you're looking for a Georgian last name, look for something that ends with -shvili.

The боевик

That's singular. Plural is "боевики". You might want to use "братки" (plural of "браток", a member of Bratva).
Also, "Warriors" is not quite the correct translation. "Боевик" does not have a "noble fighter" connotation to it, unlike "warrior". This term is mostly used to describe terrorists or criminal combatants.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's at least one contemporary document that uses them, it's a AAR with a lot of realistic details about something that happened during the current unpleasantness in Ukraine. I don't recall the title.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really people ought to use codenames for this stuff. That is what they are for. We wouldn't be having this conversation if we were talking about PEARL TALON.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Main Intelligence Directorate is a military intelligence agency that is part of the Russian Army and not a civilian agency (unlike the KGB, which was dissolved and replaced).

Really? This may change how GRU-P works in the Foundation-verse… Many actually imagined it to have gone the way of the KGB…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: True, but with that level of information, you probably already know enough to identify them with or without a name. (At least well enough for a gangster-karcist to start putting bullets in low ranking minions, anyway)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not officially, but that is effectively nearly everyone's headcanon of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is the Red Death supposed to be 610?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this a lot. Detailed yet still somewhat mysterious, blood, death and mutilation without splatter, a conflict between Horror A and Horror B - and a truly intrepid undercover agent reporting on what he sees and hears.

Poor 'Myshkin' - he was (?) a prince of a guy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And GRU was basically gone with the fall of the USSR.

This is incorrect, actually. The Main Intelligence Directorate is a military intelligence agency that is part of the Russian Army and not a civilian agency (unlike the KGB, which was dissolved and replaced). Since the Army was kept intact and largely not reorganized by the Russian Federation the GRU is still there and still doing the same job (and is actually more active and significant than either of the civilian intelligence agencies of the RF).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mikhail was intended to be used as a sort of "avatar of Orok". I prefer to keep his fate unknown. Originally, he was going to play a much bigger role but I ended up cutting a lot. At the same time, I really liked the imagery he gave and felt he added another layer of mystery, so kept that part.

More or less, the crones wanted Mikhail to become Karcist; one of the reasons Otari staged his coup.

Reviving Orok would be too… unpredictable. An inconvenience. Otari had more profitable and self-serving ideas in mind.

item.title: For the record
item.content:encoded: That photo is (derived from a color photo of) Bulgarian gangster Georgi Iliev; the color version was on the Wikimedia Commons and labeled Creative Commons, but was subsequently deleted because of a whole shitload of circumstantial evidence indicating that the license was invalid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll correct that in the hub. Forgot to change it. And GRU was basically gone with the fall of the USSR.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Russia (and the Soviet Union before it) are UN member states, GRU-P may be part of the GOC.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty sure GRU-P actually died when the USSR fell, and let the foundation/GOC take over.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I found an inconsistency between this skip and the Sarkicism hub. According to the hub, the Foundation got wind of Black Lodge in 1998; It was in the early 1990s here.

I love this skip. It also strikes me that how hard the GRU-P must have fallen in the '90s - the Foundation and GOC went picnic in Moscow, of all place, and it didn't get mentioned at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ehhhh… I guess that's a tough one. "It's purple." "It's hairy." "It's plastic." "It's metal." "It's glass." We're describing something with all of those, but I guess in technicality the proper way to say the last three would be "it's made of metal/glass/etc." even though no one speaks that way in informal English. So yeah, given that glass is actually a noun, "glass vial" is two nouns crammed together as one noun with small modifying it, and the comma is indeed redundant if we're going with formal English. So yeah, you're right.

Edit: Because I care too much about grammar for my own good, I was talking to WrongJohnSilver in detail about this and he pointed out that glass is, in this case, an attributive noun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Isn't glass vial all one noun? Not a native english speaker, but wouldn't it be better without commas altogether?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Redundant comma

"Small" and "glass" are both adjectives modifying the noun "vial," so no, it's technically correct.

Also, Sunny's drawing overlaps with the second log.

It does that because it's a transparent-background .png, I think. It could be fixed by putting a white background behind the image in Photoshop or Paint or whatever other one. (If you want that, Meta, I'll do it for you real quick.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Normally sold in small, glass vials,

Redundant comma

cock sucking

Is missing an hyphen

brain dead.

Same

Footnote 6 also jumps straight from generally explaining Sarkicism to referencing Proto- and Neo-sarkicism, without explaining either.

Also, Sunny's drawing overlaps with the second log.

Other than that, I really like this, especially the implications about an older, better form of Proto-sarkicism, the Red Death (SCP-610?), the theft of Orok's glands, and Mikhail. Did the Foundation ever contain him? What was the ritual he participated in? An attempt to revive Orok? Or the opposite, was it meant to keep him dormant?

Overall, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, the four saints of Ion. They are still lurking on the Earth, somewhere underground.

We have two of them, will there be more?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh, so Orok is still viable for a resurection. The Foundy has now control of the site fortunately.

SO S IS ALIVE :DDD
I'd really like to him in a tale at least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What became of Mikhail I wonder..? Same fate as the crones perhaps..?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Orok is "alive", biologically speaking - and could be revived (which is what the "crones" wanted to do; a reason they were killed in Otari's takeover of the group). The young will devour the old ;p

I like to think that Agent S is still alive, and still *changed*… fighting a one man battle against the group; all secret agent/beast-man style :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, forgot to add this - credit to SunnyClockwork for the skull symbol! :)

Edit: Also, I removed the image of Otari - possible copyright issue. Edit 2: Er, nevermind. Apparently it is okay.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Im always glad to see another GOI get used. and with more and more SCPs.

+1 all day any day

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: First letter, number of letters and corroborating details actually can do a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh so we now have Orok too (too bad hes dead… Lovataar is alive IIRC). I'll be waiting for Saarn and Nadox (and Ion for the "finale"? 2480 was a huge tease…)
Excelent quality as always. +1
Kinda sad the agent went MIA (pretty much 100% chance of dead). I grew to like him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really nicely done! I especially like how you characterized the agent and Five. My only real complaint here is that a fair amount of the text seems to be taken verbatim from the Sarkicism Hub, which makes it somewhat repetitive if you've read the hub before. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What Aiden said, just wanted to make sure the reader didn't think there were several blacked out agents.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A guide to Sarkic stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because it makes it easier to identify said agent, not to mention in-universe nobody would be able to identify an agent based on their last name's initial alone?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can someone link me something for me to get the basics of the Sarkic cult thingy? I'm not too sure what they're about and I would like to learn more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
O my goodness, I like this very very much. Not only does it boast quite a bit of world/lore building, but it's also entertaining as hell. One thing though:

Agent S████

Why did you include the 's'? Why not just mark over the whole thing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gaaaaaaawddammit, Meta.

Now that you've put in Russian gangsters, I have to write the obligatory James Bond crossover, mumble mumble argh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You've outdone yourself, Meta.
Got me painting another thing what is disturbing. X3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Truth be told, that was my favorite part to write and a reason I originally considered (or reconsidered) making this a tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very enjoyable, definitely a good read.

I never knew anomalous Russian gangsters would make a good SCP.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sarkicism has never been my cup of tea; I've always enjoyed your skips in spite of them. This is no different - I much prefer the exploration of the anomalous crime culture to their (admittedly deeply entwined) variant on Sarkic culture.
